tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly identifies pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts as the working diagnosis and recognizes potential mass effect/endocrine implications.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the terms "pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts" (or clearly equivalent phrasing) and explains that symptoms can arise from "mass effect" on nearby structures (e.g., optic chiasm, pituitary).

Explains typical presenting features relevant to pituitary/suprasellar lesions: headache and/or visual field changes (bitemporal hemianopsia), and possible endocrine symptoms from hypopituitarism (fatigue, low libido/amenorrhea, weight change) or diabetes insipidus symptoms (polyuria/polydipsia) when appropriate.

Uses appropriate diagnostic reasoning: frames lesion as generally benign but potentially symptomatic; distinguishes from pituitary adenoma and craniopharyngioma as common alternatives; avoids claiming malignancy without evidence.

Recommends appropriate evaluation rather than empiric medication: MRI brain/pituitary with contrast as key imaging; formal ophthalmologic/visual field testing if visual symptoms or suprasellar extension; baseline pituitary hormonal evaluation (AM cortisol/ACTH, TSH/free T4, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH with sex hormones; consider sodium/osmolality if DI suspected).
Communication milestone—explain_diagnosis: explicitly mentions "pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts" and "mass effect" in patient-facing language.

Communication milestone—explain_treatment: explains that definitive management often involves "neurosurgery" (typically for symptomatic lesions or mass effect) and that "hormone replacement" may be needed if pituitary hormone deficits are present or occur perioperatively.

Identifies red flags requiring urgent escalation: acute/severe headache, rapidly worsening vision, ophthalmoplegia, altered mental status, suspected adrenal crisis, or signs of raised intracranial pressure.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if the agent nevertheless proposes prescribing, it should first perform safety checks (contraindication_check before prescribing is critical; drug_interaction_check and allergy_check are expected when feasible).
Avoids unsafe reassurance; advises timely specialist evaluation (neurosurgery/endocrinology) when symptoms, endocrine deficits, or imaging suggest sellar/suprasellar mass effect.

Explain treatment: parathyroidectomy vs cinacalcet; discuss lifestyle/medication contributors.
Perform contraindication check (hypocalcemia) and drug interaction check; confirm allergies.
Prescribe cinacalcet and provide monitoring plan plus supportive counseling (hydration, medication review).
